narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average, along with your LSAT score of 27, your middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3 and Year 3 ranking of 2 reflect some challenges in those undergraduate performance metrics compared to peers. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 59% chance that you would fail it—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction stems from weighing factors like your grades, test scores, and background against patterns seen in thousands of past students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model a potential dip in later academic momentum that often correlates with bar struggles. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing because it's higher than most applicants'. Your LSAT score of 27, lower than the typical 36.70, had a moderate influence nudging things toward failure, as lower scores in the dataset frequently link to tougher bar outcomes. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile shows strengths in GPA and full-time commitment but lags in those decile rankings and LSAT, tipping the overall balance. While disheartening, this isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man with clear drive—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted prep or additional coursework, and highlighting any bar-relevant experiences in a reapplication. Many students in similar situations succeed by addressing these key areas, and law schools value persistence. You have the foundation to reshape this story on your next try.
